questions.q: An armed conflict breaks out in a region where your project's only certified component supplier operates. Deliveries have not yet stopped. What should the PM do FIRST?
opts: Wait until deliveries actually stop before reacting
Assess the impact on the project and update the risk register with response options
Immediately switch to an uncertified supplier to be safe
Escalate to the sponsor and pause procurement
questions.exp: Geopolitical events are external business environment changes the PM must survey and assess. Nothing has failed yet, so this is still a risk: analyze the exposure, update the register, and prepare responses (alternate sourcing, buffer stock) before it becomes an issue. Waiting is passive; switching suppliers or escalating before analysis skips assessment.

questions.q: Mid-release, a competitor launches a generative-AI capability that makes two epics on your product roadmap far less valuable to customers. What should happen NEXT?
opts: Finish the epics as planned since they were committed to stakeholders
Work with the product owner to reassess backlog priorities against the changed market
Cancel the release and start a new project
Add an identical AI capability to the current sprint
questions.exp: Agile teams continually review the external environment for impacts on the backlog. A market shift changes the value of planned work, so the PO reprioritizes based on current value — not sunk commitments. Cancelling is extreme, and injecting unrefined work into the current sprint violates sprint scope protection.

questions.q: A new AI service could automate a data-migration deliverable your hybrid project planned to build manually over four months. The technology is unproven in your organization. What is the BEST approach?
opts: Adopt the AI service immediately to capture the schedule savings
Ignore it — the baseline is approved and changes add risk
Run a time-boxed spike to evaluate the technology, then bring a recommendation through change control
Ask the vendor for a guarantee before considering it
questions.exp: New technology is an external environment change worth evaluating, but PMI expects evidence before commitment. A time-boxed spike (an agile technique) generates data, and the resulting scope/schedule change still flows through the project's governance. Immediate adoption skips analysis; ignoring it ignores potential value.

questions.q: Interest rates rise sharply and the finance director warns that the cost of capital assumption in your project's business case is no longer valid. What should the PM do?
opts: Continue — financing is an organizational concern, not a project concern
Reassess the business case viability with the sponsor and present options
Cut project scope immediately to reduce cost
Suspend all spending until rates fall
questions.exp: The PM is responsible for surveying external economic changes and assessing their effect on project value. If the business case assumptions break, the sponsor owns the business case decision — the PM's job is to quantify the impact and present options (continue, descope, pause, cancel). Unilateral cuts or freezes exceed the PM's authority.

questions.q: Customer interviews during a release show that demand has shifted: users now want mobile self-service, and a third of your remaining backlog targets a declining desktop workflow. What should the team do?
opts: Deliver the remaining backlog first, then consider mobile in the next release
Have the product owner reprioritize the backlog based on the new market evidence
Escalate to the steering committee for a scope decision
Split the team to work on both workflows equally
questions.exp: Market shifts are exactly why agile keeps the backlog adaptive. The product owner reorders the backlog using the freshest value evidence — that is normal agile governance, not an escalation event. Finishing low-value work first destroys value; splitting focus without prioritization avoids the decision.

questions.q: An industry standards body publishes a new interoperability standard that your delivered product will eventually need to meet, though no customer requires it yet. What should the PM do?
opts: Add the standard to the scope baseline immediately
Record it as a risk/watch item, assess the impact and timing, and monitor it
Ignore it until a customer demands compliance
Ask the team to informally build toward the standard where convenient
questions.exp: An emerging external change that is not yet binding is monitored, not automatically implemented. The PM assesses potential impact, records it on the watchlist/risk register, and reviews it periodically. Adding scope without a driver is gold plating; informal 'building toward it' is uncontrolled scope change.

questions.q: How should an agile team make reviewing the external business environment (regulations, technology, market moves) a routine practice rather than a one-time event?
opts: Assign it to the PMO annually
Build environmental scanning into recurring cadences such as backlog refinement and release planning
Wait for stakeholders to raise external changes at sprint reviews
Add an environmental-scan story to one sprint per year
questions.exp: The 2026 ECO calls for continually reviewing the external environment for backlog impact. Embedding scanning into existing cadences — refinement, release planning, roadmap reviews — makes it continuous and connects findings directly to prioritization. Annual or reactive approaches leave long blind spots.

questions.q: New trade sanctions make it illegal to continue purchasing from a vendor that supplies a critical subsystem, effective in 60 days. What should the PM do FIRST?
opts: Ask the vendor to route shipments through a third country
Assess the impact with legal/compliance and develop response options for governance review
Immediately terminate the contract for convenience
Accelerate remaining orders before the deadline to stockpile
questions.exp: Legal changes require assessment with the right experts before action. The PM works with legal and compliance to understand obligations and options (alternate sourcing, redesign, schedule change), then routes the decision through governance. Circumventing sanctions is an ethics violation; stockpiling may itself breach the sanctions' intent and terms.

questions.q: A draft regulation that would affect your product's data handling is under public consultation and may pass in a year. Your release ships in six months. What should the team do?
opts: Redesign the product now to meet the draft in full
Track the regulation as an assumption/risk, design for flexibility where cheap, and reassess each release planning cycle
Delay the release until the regulation is final
Assume it will not pass since it is only a draft
questions.exp: Emerging regulation is uncertain — treat it as a monitored risk. Building cheap flexibility (options thinking) and reassessing at every planning cadence balances responsiveness with avoiding speculative rework. Full redesign or delay overreacts to an unfinalized rule; assuming it dies ignores a surveyed threat.

questions.q: Your team is three sprints into hand-building a recommendation engine when a mature open-source model is released that meets most of the need at a fraction of the effort. Team members want to keep building because of the work already invested. What should the PM/coach do?
opts: Support the team's preference — morale matters most
Facilitate an objective comparison of value and remaining cost, ignoring sunk cost, and let the PO decide direction
Mandate an immediate switch to the open-source model
Ask the sponsor to decide
questions.exp: This is a technology shift in the external environment plus a sunk-cost trap. PMI expects decisions on remaining cost versus value, not effort already spent. The PM facilitates the analysis; the product owner owns the value decision. Mandating a switch or deferring to the sponsor bypasses the empowered team structure.

questions.q: A national election in a country hosting your project's rollout could reverse the regulatory approval your project depends on. The vote is in four months. What is the BEST action?
opts: Pause the project until after the election
Perform scenario analysis, define contingency plans and triggers, and brief governance on the exposure
Lobby stakeholders to influence the election outcome
Proceed as planned — politics is outside project scope
questions.exp: Geopolitical uncertainty is managed like any other risk: model scenarios, prepare contingency responses with defined triggers, and keep governance informed. Pausing surrenders four months on a possibility; ignoring politics contradicts the ECO's explicit requirement to survey geopolitical changes.

questions.q: A sudden labor-market shift means the specialized engineers your next two releases depend on are being hired away, and recruiting lead times have tripled. What should the team do FIRST?
opts: Raise salaries for the current team without approval
Assess the impact on the release plan and backlog, then develop options such as cross-training, sequencing changes, or contracting
Reduce quality standards so existing staff can move faster
Report the problem to HR and continue the current plan
questions.exp: Market changes include talent markets. The PM/team first assesses what the shift does to planned capacity and value delivery, then builds options — cross-training (growing T-shaped skills), resequencing the backlog, or supplementing capacity. Compensation actions exceed PM authority, and cutting quality trades a visible problem for a hidden one.

questions.q: Halfway through your project, a company-wide reorganization replaces the functional managers who committed resources to you. Your team members now report to leaders unfamiliar with the project. What should the PM do FIRST?
opts: Continue with existing resource commitments — they were formally approved
Reassess the stakeholder landscape and engage the new managers to reconfirm commitments
Escalate to the sponsor to enforce the original agreements
Rebaseline the schedule assuming resource loss
questions.exp: Organizational change directly affects the project. The PM evaluates the impact and acts: update the stakeholder register, build relationships with the new managers, and reconfirm resource commitments. Old approvals do not bind people who never made them; escalating or rebaselining before engaging skips assessment and relationship-building.

questions.q: After a merger, your integration project team is drawn from both legacy companies. One group escalates everything in writing; the other resolves issues informally. Friction is growing. What should the PM do?
opts: Impose the acquiring company's norms since it owns the merged entity
Assess both cultures and facilitate the team in agreeing shared working norms
Separate the two groups into independent workstreams
Report the friction to HR as a personnel issue
questions.exp: Assessing organizational culture is an explicit 2026 ECO enabler. The PM diagnoses the cultural difference and facilitates a shared team agreement (working norms/charter) rather than declaring a winner. Segregating the groups entrenches the divide; outsourcing it to HR abdicates the PM's team-leadership responsibility.

questions.q: Your organization announces a shift from a functional structure to a projectized structure over the next six months. How does this MOST affect your in-flight project?
opts: No effect — structure changes only affect future projects
PM authority, resource ownership, and team reporting lines will change and must be assessed and planned for
The project must restart under the new structure
Only the PMO is affected
questions.exp: Organizational structure determines the PM's authority and how resources are assigned. Moving toward projectized increases PM authority and gives the project dedicated resources — but the transition itself creates stakeholder, staffing, and process impacts the PM must evaluate and manage, per the ECO's 'support organizational change' task.

questions.q: Your hybrid project will replace a tool that 400 employees use daily. The technical work is on track, but there is no plan for preparing users for the transition. What should the PM do?
opts: Nothing — adoption is the business owner's problem after handover
Work with the sponsor and business leads to build organizational change management (communication, training, readiness) into the project plan
Extend the pilot indefinitely until users feel ready
Send an announcement email at go-live
questions.exp: Delivering outputs without adoption fails to deliver value. Supporting organizational change — assessing readiness, communicating, training, and managing resistance — is part of the Business Environment domain, planned with the sponsor and business leads, not bolted on at go-live.

questions.q: Your organization is adopting agile, but during the transition a middle manager still demands the detailed weekly status decks the team used to produce, consuming hours of team time. What should the Scrum Master do?
opts: Refuse — status decks are not part of Scrum
Understand the manager's underlying information need and work to satisfy it through agile artifacts like the increment, boards, and reviews
Have the team keep producing the decks to avoid conflict
Escalate the manager to the transformation office
questions.exp: During organizational change, people cling to old mechanisms because they meet real needs. The servant leader uncovers the need (visibility, predictability) and meets it with transparent agile information radiators and invitations to sprint reviews. Flat refusal breeds resistance; quiet compliance perpetuates waste; escalation is premature.

questions.q: You are tailoring the delivery approach for a new initiative in a division known for strict hierarchies, formal signoffs, and low risk tolerance. What should you do FIRST?
opts: Impose a pure agile approach to modernize the division
Assess the organizational culture and constraints, then tailor an approach that can succeed in that context
Use the most predictive approach possible to match the hierarchy
Ask PMI which methodology is correct
questions.exp: Assessing organizational culture is a named 2026 enabler, and tailoring means fitting the approach to context — team, product, and organization. Culture may point toward more ceremony and incremental agile adoption, but the assessment comes before the choice. Imposing either extreme without assessment is anti-tailoring.

questions.q: Your project sponsor departs the company. The replacement sponsor questions why the project exists and favors a different strategic priority. What should the PM do FIRST?
opts: Continue executing — the charter authorizes the work regardless of sponsor
Meet the new sponsor to walk through the business case, current status, and expected benefits, and understand their concerns
Ask the PMO to overrule the new sponsor
Slow the project down until direction is clear
questions.exp: A sponsor change is an organizational change with direct project impact. The PM proactively engages the new sponsor: re-present the business case and benefits, listen to their strategic concerns, and rebuild alignment. Hiding behind the charter or routing around the sponsor damages the project's most important relationship.

questions.q: The company announces layoffs will be decided next quarter. Your agile team's throughput drops as members quietly job-hunt and avoid ambitious work. What should the PM/Scrum Master do?
opts: Promise the team no one will be cut
Acknowledge the uncertainty honestly, get leaders to share what is known, and re-establish a realistic near-term focus with the team
Add buffer to all estimates and report lower velocity as the new normal
Discipline members whose output dropped
questions.exp: Organizational change creates fear that no process fixes. Servant leadership means honest transparency about what is and isn't known, advocating for timely communication from leadership, and helping the team find achievable focus. False promises destroy trust; silently accepting decline or punishing symptoms ignores the cause.

questions.q: During initiation, you are establishing project governance. Which set BEST captures what the governance framework should define?
opts: The detailed WBS, activity list, and network diagram
Decision-making structure, roles, reporting cadence, escalation paths and thresholds, and success metrics
The team's daily standup format and story point scale
Vendor payment schedules and invoice templates
questions.exp: Governance defines how the project is directed and controlled: who decides what, within which thresholds, how performance is reported, how issues escalate, and how success is measured — drawing on organizational process assets. WBS detail and team ceremonies are planning and delivery mechanics, not governance.

questions.q: A vendor issue will add $40,000 in cost. Your governance plan gives the PM authority for impacts up to $25,000, with the steering committee above that. The vendor demands an answer today. What should the PM do?
opts: Approve it — the deadline justifies acting beyond the threshold
Follow the escalation path: brief the steering committee (or its emergency mechanism) with analysis and a recommendation
Split the cost into two $20,000 approvals to stay under the threshold
Delay the vendor until the next scheduled committee meeting without communicating
questions.exp: Escalation thresholds exist precisely for this moment. The PM escalates with a completed impact analysis and recommendation, using whatever urgent-decision route governance provides. Exceeding authority or structuring approvals to evade thresholds violates governance integrity; silent delay damages the vendor relationship and the schedule.

questions.q: Leadership worries that an empowered agile team means 'no governance.' How does governance actually work in a well-run agile delivery?
opts: It doesn't — agile teams are exempt from governance
Governance is lightweight and built into cadences: transparent backlogs, definition of done, sprint reviews with stakeholders, and clear decision rights like the PO owning priority
The Scrum Master approves all decisions as the governance authority
Every story requires steering committee signoff
questions.exp: Agile governance shifts from gate documents to transparency and cadence: working agreements, DoD as a quality policy, reviews as recurring checkpoints, and explicit decision rights (PO for value, team for how). Empowerment operates inside those guardrails — it is different governance, not absent governance.

questions.q: A senior executive directly instructs one of your team members to swap a component in the approved design, skipping change control. You learn of it before work begins. What should the PM do?
opts: Let the work proceed — the executive outranks the PM
Speak with the executive, explain the impact and the change process, and route the request through integrated change control
Tell the team member to ignore all executive requests
Quietly update the design documents to match the instruction
questions.exp: Governance applies to everyone, including executives. The PM addresses it directly and respectfully: capture the request, assess impacts, and submit it through change control before work starts. Allowing it creates an uncontrolled baseline; papering over it corrupts project records; blanket refusal poisons the relationship.

questions.q: At kickoff you realize the charter says the project should 'improve customer experience' but defines no measurable success criteria. What should the PM do?
opts: Start executing — success can be defined at closing
Work with the sponsor and key stakeholders to define measurable success metrics and get them agreed and documented
Invent metrics unilaterally and put them in the plan
Treat on-time, on-budget delivery as the success definition
questions.exp: Defining success metrics is an explicit governance enabler in the 2026 ECO. Without agreed measures, every stakeholder judges success differently and benefits can't be evaluated. Metrics are agreed with the sponsor/stakeholders, not invented by the PM alone — and delivery performance is not the same as business success.

questions.q: A PMO health check finds your hybrid project skips the organization's required stage-gate reviews because 'we do sprint reviews instead.' What is the BEST response?
opts: Keep skipping gates — sprint reviews are superior
Work with the PMO to map how the project's agile cadences satisfy gate intent, and hold the gates that organizational governance still requires
Abandon sprint reviews and run gates only
Ask the sponsor to exempt the project from all PMO oversight
questions.exp: Tailoring adapts governance but does not ignore organizational requirements. In hybrid delivery, the PM aligns agile cadences with mandated gates — often the same evidence serves both — and complies where governance genuinely requires a gate decision. Unilateral exemption from oversight is anti-governance.

questions.q: Analysis shows your project can no longer achieve one of the strategic objectives stated in its charter. Who has the authority to change a charter-level objective?
opts: The project manager, since they manage the project
The sponsor/initiating authority, through the governance process
The project team by consensus
The change control board handles all changes including the charter
questions.exp: The charter is issued by the sponsor or initiating authority, and only that level can change what the project is chartered to achieve. The PM's role is to surface the analysis and recommend options. A CCB governs changes to baselines within the project; chartered objectives sit above it.

questions.q: Your hybrid program has predictive infrastructure workstreams and agile software teams. Stakeholders complain that decisions stall because no one knows which forum decides what. What should the PM do?
opts: Route every decision to the steering committee to be safe
Define and communicate a decision-rights matrix: which decisions belong to teams, the PO, the PM, and the steering committee, with escalation thresholds
Let each workstream decide independently
Hold more meetings until decisions emerge
questions.exp: Hybrid delivery fails most often at the seams. Governance must explicitly outline decision rights and escalation paths across both worlds — what teams self-manage, what value calls the PO makes, and what crosses thresholds to the committee. Centralizing everything creates a bottleneck; full independence creates chaos.

questions.q: Two weeks before launch, a penetration test reveals a vulnerability that violates your organization's security compliance standard. Fixing it will delay launch by three weeks. The sponsor suggests launching anyway and patching later. What should the PM do?
opts: Follow the sponsor's instruction — they own the decision
Present the noncompliance consequences (legal, contractual, breach exposure) and route the launch decision through the proper governance/compliance authority
Launch quietly and schedule the patch
Cancel the launch on their own authority
questions.exp: Analyzing the consequences of noncompliance is a named ECO enabler. Security compliance failures carry organizational risk beyond the sponsor's individual authority, so the PM ensures the decision is made transparently at the right level — compliance/security governance — with full impact information. Silent launch is an ethics violation.

questions.q: Your program's outputs feed the company's public ESG disclosures, and a new sustainability reporting rule requires auditable emissions data from projects like yours starting next year. What should the PM do?
opts: Treat it as a corporate reporting problem outside the program
Confirm the compliance requirement, classify it, and build the data-capture and verification work into the program's plans and backlogs
Estimate emissions figures informally when asked
Object to the requirement as outside the original scope
questions.exp: The 2026 ECO explicitly lists sustainability among compliance requirements to confirm and manage. Regulatory ESG reporting needs auditable data, which means real project work — captured through change control in predictive streams and backlog items in agile ones. Informal estimates create legal exposure.

questions.q: During planning, how should the PM handle the variety of compliance obligations (security, health and safety, environmental, regulatory) that apply to the project?
opts: Address compliance topics as they surface during execution
Identify and classify the compliance categories, assess threats to compliance, and plan the approach and measures for each
Delegate all compliance work to the legal department
List them in the charter and consider it done
questions.exp: The ECO sequence is deliberate: confirm requirements, classify categories, determine threats, plan methods and actions, and measure compliance. Proactive classification during planning drives who is engaged, what controls exist, and how compliance is verified. Reactive handling and delegation both leave the project accountable but blind.

questions.q: Visiting a construction site, you see a subcontractor crew working without the fall-protection equipment your project's safety plan mandates. What should the PM do FIRST?
opts: Note it for discussion at the weekly vendor meeting
Have the unsafe work stopped immediately, then address the compliance failure with the subcontractor through the proper process
Photograph it for the claims file and continue the visit
Warn the crew informally and move on
questions.exp: Health and safety is the one compliance area where immediate intervention precedes process: imminent danger to people is stopped first. Then the PM treats it as a compliance breach — root cause, corrective action, contract implications, and monitoring. Deferring or documenting-only tolerates ongoing risk to life.

questions.q: To hit a sprint goal, your team proposes skipping the mandated accessibility check on new screens, arguing 'we can audit later before release.' What should the Scrum Master do?
opts: Allow it — the team owns how it works
Remind the team that compliance standards are part of the definition of done and facilitate a scope conversation with the PO instead
Report the team to the compliance office
Extend the sprint so both can be done
questions.exp: Compliance requirements belong in the definition of done precisely so they can't be traded away under pressure. Team autonomy covers how work gets done, not whether mandated standards apply. The honest move is negotiating scope with the PO, not accumulating invisible compliance debt. Sprints are never extended.

questions.q: Your agile team discovers that a planned feature would move customer data to a cloud region prohibited by data-residency law in a target market. The feature is top of next sprint's backlog. What should happen?
opts: Build it and restrict the market later if regulators complain
Flag the compliance threat now, engage privacy/legal, and rework or reprioritize the feature so the solution is compliant before it is built
Ship it only to unregulated markets without telling the PO why
Remove the feature permanently
questions.exp: Determining threats to compliance and acting before violation is the point of compliance management. Raising it immediately, involving the right experts, and adjusting the backlog keeps delivery legal and preserves value — prevention over remediation. Building first and hoping is knowingly creating noncompliance.

questions.q: An audit finds that a key vendor on your program has been misreporting its sustainability certifications, which your contract and your client's ESG commitments require. What should the PM do FIRST?
opts: Terminate the vendor immediately
Verify the findings, assess the compliance and contractual impact, and engage procurement/legal on the response
Ask the vendor to backdate corrected certificates
Conceal the finding until after the next client review
questions.exp: Facts first, then proportionate action through the right channels: confirm the audit result, analyze consequences of the noncompliance (contractual breach, client commitments, reputational risk), and involve procurement and legal in remedies from cure notices to termination. Backdating or concealment are ethics violations.

questions.q: Leadership asks, 'How do we know your project is actually compliant, not just planning to be?' What BEST answers this ongoing need?
opts: A signed statement from the PM each quarter
Defined compliance measures tracked through audits, checklists, and reporting — showing the extent of compliance over time
The absence of regulator complaints so far
A clause passing all compliance liability to vendors
questions.exp: 'Measure the extent to which the project is in compliance' is a named enabler: it takes objective mechanisms — compliance registers, audit results, control checklists, trend reporting — not assurances or silence. Liability transfer doesn't create compliance, and no-news is not evidence.

questions.q: Midway through a sprint, a marketing stakeholder asks the team to change how a feature under construction works. The change is genuinely valuable. What is the correct flow?
opts: The team implements it immediately — agile welcomes change
The request goes to the product owner, who assesses value and orders it in the backlog; the current sprint's scope stays protected unless the PO and team renegotiate
The stakeholder must submit a change request to the CCB
The Scrum Master decides whether to accept it
questions.exp: Agile welcomes change between sprints, through the backlog, and through the PO — that IS the change control process in adaptive delivery. Mid-sprint scope is protected to preserve the sprint goal; only the PO with the team can renegotiate it. CCBs govern predictive baselines, not sprint backlogs.

questions.q: A stakeholder submitted a change request three weeks ago and has heard nothing. The CCB deferred it pending analysis. What should the PM have been doing?
opts: Nothing until the CCB decides
Communicating the status of the proposed change to the requester throughout — received, under analysis, deferred, and why
Telling the stakeholder it was probably rejected
Letting the CCB chair handle all communication
questions.exp: 'Communicate the status of proposed changes' is an explicit enabler. Every change request deserves a transparent lifecycle: acknowledgment, analysis status, decision, and rationale. Silence erodes stakeholder trust and invites workarounds. The PM owns this communication regardless of who chairs the board.

questions.q: A change was approved and implemented, but months later the team discovers the requirements documents and test cases still reflect the old design, causing rework. What was missed?
opts: The change should have been rejected
Updating project documentation and artifacts as part of implementing the approved change
More CCB meetings
Assigning blame to the team member who implemented it
questions.exp: Change control isn't finished at 'approved' or even 'built' — the ECO explicitly includes implementing approved changes AND updating project documentation to reflect them. Stale artifacts silently poison downstream work: testing, training, operations, and future changes all inherit the inconsistency.

questions.q: In your hybrid project, a single customer request affects both the fixed-price hardware baseline and the agile software backlog. How should the change be managed?
opts: Handle the hardware part through change control and quietly absorb the software part
Assess the full impact once, route the baseline impact through integrated change control, and coordinate the software portion through backlog prioritization — keeping both decisions consistent
Apply the strictest process (CCB) to both halves
Let each team handle its half independently without coordination
questions.exp: Hybrid change management uses each track's proper mechanism but coordinates them: one integrated impact assessment, CCB approval for the baselined scope, PO prioritization for the adaptive scope, and aligned communication. Splitting the change without coordination is how hybrid projects deliver mismatched halves.

questions.q: On a call, the customer verbally approves your proposed scope change and asks the team to start right away. What should the PM do?
opts: Start work — verbal approval from the customer is sufficient
Obtain the formal approval required by the change control process before implementing, and confirm the conversation in writing
Refuse to discuss changes outside CCB meetings
Start work but keep it off the status reports until papers are signed
questions.exp: Executing the change control process means changes are approved through the defined mechanism, not conversation. The PM confirms in writing and secures formal approval before work begins — protecting both parties contractually and keeping baselines defensible. Working 'off the books' is a governance and ethics failure.

questions.q: Your team faces three simultaneous blockers: a test environment outage stopping all work, a pending architecture decision affecting next sprint, and a slow laptop annoying one developer. How should the Scrum Master handle them?
opts: Work them in the order they were reported
Prioritize by impact — restore the environment blocking everyone first, drive the architecture decision next, and queue the laptop fix
Delegate all three to the team to self-manage
Escalate all three to management immediately
questions.exp: The ECO calls for evaluating the impact of impediments and prioritizing them. Impediment triage mirrors risk thinking: breadth and severity of impact drive order. Escalation is a tool for impediments beyond the SM's reach, not a default; ignoring prioritization wastes the team's most constrained resource — flow.

questions.q: A risk in your register — 'key regulatory approval may be delayed' — has just materialized: the regulator announced a 60-day delay. What should the PM do FIRST?
opts: Keep managing it as a risk since it was already registered
Recognize it is now an issue, log it in the issue log, and execute/adapt the planned response with the relevant stakeholders
Remove it from the risk register and move on
Open a new risk for a possible further delay only
questions.exp: When a risk's trigger occurs, it becomes an issue — certain, present, and needing action rather than probability management. The ECO enabler is 'recognize when a risk becomes an issue.' Log the issue, activate the prepared response, collaborate with stakeholders on resolution, and reassess residual and secondary risks.

questions.q: Your team has been blocked for a week because another department's API team won't prioritize the integration your sprint needs. The Scrum Master's peer-level requests have failed. What should happen NEXT?
opts: The team should build a permanent workaround and drop the dependency silently
Escalate the impediment through the agreed path with clear impact data so leadership can arbitrate priorities
Wait — the API team will get to it eventually
Report the API team's manager to HR
questions.exp: Servant leaders resolve what they can and escalate what they cannot — after genuine attempts, with evidence of impact. Cross-department priority conflicts are exactly what governance escalation paths exist to arbitrate. Waiting extends the blockage; silent workarounds may create untracked scope and technical debt.

questions.q: For the fourth consecutive sprint, deployments are delayed because the release approval step takes days. Each time, the team treats it as a one-off annoyance. What should the Scrum Master do?
opts: Keep raising each delay as it happens
Treat the recurrence as a systemic impediment: analyze the root cause of the approval bottleneck and pursue a durable fix with the process owners
Tell the team to plan around the delay permanently
Skip the approval step
questions.exp: The ECO requires continually reassessing whether impediments are truly being addressed. A recurring blocker is a system problem, not bad luck — root-cause it (why does approval take days?) and fix the process with its owners, perhaps via automation or delegated authority. Normalizing the delay institutionalizes waste; bypassing approvals breaks governance.

questions.q: Your risk register lists 'inadequate IT security on the vendor-hosted platform' as a high risk. Which action BEST executes the risk management plan for this risk?
opts: Note the risk in monthly reports until something happens
Implement the planned response — e.g., require security controls in the contract, conduct penetration testing, and track findings to closure — and report status to stakeholders
Accept the risk since security is the vendor's job
Buy cyber insurance and consider it fully handled
questions.exp: The ECO explicitly cites executing risk responses for security. A plan without implemented responses is theater: contractual controls, verification (pen tests), and tracked remediation actually reduce exposure, and communicating risk status keeps stakeholders informed. Insurance transfers some financial impact but leaves the operational risk untreated.

questions.q: Your program identifies a sustainability risk: a planned material may be restricted by upcoming environmental rules, and its production emissions could breach a client commitment. How should this be managed?
opts: Ignore it until the rules are final
Enter it in the risk register, analyze probability and impact, and plan responses such as alternative materials or design changes — reviewing it at both governance boards and team planning cadences
Treat sustainability topics separately from the risk process
Transfer it by adding a disclaimer to the contract
questions.exp: The 2026 ECO names managing sustainability risks within risk management — same register, same analysis, same response planning, visible in both hybrid governance layers. Segregating ESG risks from the process makes them invisible to decision-makers; disclaimers rarely transfer regulatory or reputational exposure.

questions.q: A major risk's probability has doubled since last month due to vendor instability. Executives will decide next week whether to commit further funding. What must the PM ensure?
opts: The risk stays internal to the team to avoid alarming executives
The changed risk exposure and its potential impact on the project are clearly communicated to the decision-makers before they commit
The register is updated but not circulated
The vendor never learns the project tracks this risk
questions.exp: 'Communicate the status of a risk impact on the project' is an explicit enabler. Decision-quality depends on current risk information — withholding a materially worsened exposure from a funding decision is both a governance failure and an ethics problem. Registers exist to inform, not to archive.

questions.q: A stakeholder claims agile teams 'don't do risk management.' Which reply BEST describes how adaptive teams actually manage risk?
opts: Correct — agile relies on luck and fast recovery
Risk is managed continuously through short iterations, prioritizing risky work early with spikes, transparency in reviews and boards, and refining the backlog as threats emerge
Risk management happens only in a dedicated sprint each quarter
The Scrum Master maintains a private risk register the team never sees
questions.exp: Agile embeds risk management in its mechanics: short feedback loops limit exposure, spikes attack uncertainty early, reviews and radiators keep threats visible, and reprioritization is the response mechanism. It is continuous and team-owned — the opposite of hidden or quarterly.

questions.q: Three months into your project you hit a vendor-onboarding problem that a project last year solved painfully. Nobody checked their lessons learned. What practice would have prevented this?
opts: Longer status meetings
Consulting the lessons learned repository during planning and throughout the project, not only writing lessons at closure
Hiring the previous project's PM
Avoiding vendors altogether
questions.exp: 'Utilize lessons learned' means consuming organizational knowledge, not just producing it. Lessons repositories are organizational process assets meant to be searched at planning and revisited during execution. Writing lessons at closure while never reading anyone else's is the most common continuous-improvement failure.

questions.q: Your team's retrospective produced a testing improvement that cut defect leakage by half. Other teams in the organization face the same problem. What should happen?
opts: Keep it as the team's competitive advantage
Share the improvement and update the relevant organizational process assets so other teams benefit
Wait for other teams to discover it independently
Sell the practice to other teams for budget
questions.exp: Continuous improvement in the 2026 ECO includes updating organizational process assets — templates, guidance, communities of practice — so learning compounds beyond one team. Hoarding improvements optimizes the team at the organization's expense, the opposite of enterprise agility.

questions.q: Your organization's 'continuous improvement process' is a form nobody has updated in five years, and teams quietly ignore it. As PM you rely on it for your project's improvement loop. What should you do?
opts: Follow the stale process anyway — process is process
Raise it with the process owner/PMO and help ensure the improvement process itself is updated to something teams actually use
Create a private process and tell no one
Declare continuous improvement impossible here
questions.exp: The ECO enabler is 'help ensure continuous improvement processes are updated' — improvement applies to the improvement mechanism itself. The PM surfaces the dysfunction to the owner and contributes to fixing it, modeling the behavior. Complying with a dead process or going rogue both leave the organization broken.

questions.q: You notice new project teams across the organization repeating mistakes your program already solved, because knowledge lives in individual heads. What is the BEST way to address this?
opts: Send a company-wide email listing every mistake
Establish knowledge-sharing mechanisms — communities of practice, searchable lessons repositories, and cross-team reviews — so learning transfers continuously
Require every PM to read all closure reports
Rotate all staff between projects every month
questions.exp: Knowledge transfer needs living mechanisms, not documents alone: communities of practice, curated repositories, demos, and cross-team retrospectives move tacit knowledge where reading mandates and one-off emails cannot. This is continuous improvement operating at the organizational level.

questions.q: Your team wants to feed the project's schedule, cost, and stakeholder data into a new external AI assistant to generate forecasts. The tool is not on the organization's approved list. What should the PM do FIRST?
opts: Allow it — the productivity gain outweighs the formality
Check the organization's AI governance and data policies, get the tool assessed/approved, and ensure no sensitive data is exposed before any use
Allow it only if the tool's results look accurate
Ban all AI use on the project
questions.exp: AI tools are subject to governance like any other: data privacy, security, IP, and approval policies apply before project data leaves the organization's control. The PM enables innovation through the compliant path — assessment and approval — rather than either shadow IT or a blanket ban. Accuracy does not cure a data leak.

questions.q: Your organization has a public commitment to cut project-related carbon emissions 30%. The fastest delivery option for your program relies on air-freighting components, which blows through the program's carbon budget. What should the PM do?
opts: Choose the fastest option — schedule always wins
Treat the carbon target as a constraint: analyze options against schedule, cost, and emissions together, and bring the trade-off with a recommendation to governance
Quietly exceed the carbon budget and offset it later without approval
Ignore the commitment since it is not a law
questions.exp: Organizational ESG commitments are genuine project constraints even when not legislated — they carry reputational, contractual, and stakeholder consequences. The PMI approach is transparent trade-off analysis across competing constraints and a governance-level decision, not unilateral optimization of one variable or hidden breaches.

flashcards.q: What three governance elements must the PM establish per the 2026 ECO's 'define and establish project governance' task?
flashcards.a:
1. Structure, rules, procedures, reporting, ethics, and policies (built from organizational process assets)
2. Success metrics — agreed and measurable, not just on-time/on-budget
3. Escalation paths and thresholds — who decides what, and at what limit an issue moves up

flashcards.q: When is escalating through the governance path the RIGHT answer on the 2026 exam?
flashcards.a:
When the decision or impact exceeds the PM's defined authority/threshold (cost, risk, cross-department priority), AND the PM has already analyzed the situation and prepared a recommendation.

Escalate WITH analysis and options — never raw problems, and never before attempting resolution at your own level.

flashcards.q: How does governance work in agile and hybrid projects?
flashcards.a:
Agile: lightweight and cadence-based — transparent backlog, definition of done, sprint reviews as checkpoints, clear decision rights (PO owns priority, team owns how).

Hybrid: map agile cadences to required organizational gates and define a decision-rights matrix across both tracks. Tailoring adapts governance — it never means ignoring it.

flashcards.q: An executive or sponsor tries to bypass change control. What is the PMI answer?
flashcards.a:
Governance applies to everyone. Respectfully explain the impact and the process, capture the request, and route it through integrated change control (or the PO/backlog in agile) BEFORE work proceeds.

Never silently comply, never quietly update documents to match, and never simply refuse without explanation.

flashcards.q: Change control isn't done when the change is approved. What two steps complete it?
flashcards.a:
1. Implement the approved change
2. Update project documentation and artifacts to reflect it (requirements, test cases, plans, registers)

Also: communicate the status of every proposed change to its requester throughout — received, under analysis, approved/deferred/rejected, and why.

flashcards.q: How does the 2026 ECO treat sustainability in compliance and risk?
flashcards.a:
Sustainability is explicitly listed as a compliance requirement to confirm (alongside security, health & safety, regulatory) and as a risk category to manage.

Treat ESG obligations as real constraints and register risks: same impact analysis, same change control/backlog flow, same governance visibility as any other requirement.

flashcards.q: What is the PMI approach when an ESG commitment (e.g., carbon target) conflicts with schedule or cost?
flashcards.a:
Treat the commitment as a constraint even if it isn't law. Analyze options against ALL constraints together (schedule, cost, emissions, reputation), then bring the trade-off with a recommendation to governance for a transparent decision.

Wrong answers: unilaterally picking speed, hiding the breach, or offsetting without approval.

flashcards.q: A team wants to use an unapproved AI tool with project data. What must happen first?
flashcards.a:
Check AI governance and data policies BEFORE any project data is shared: privacy, security, IP, and tool approval.

The PM enables the compliant path (assessment → approval → guarded use) rather than shadow IT or a blanket ban. Tool accuracy never excuses a data exposure.

flashcards.q: How is AI/new technology treated as an EXTERNAL business environment change in 2026?
flashcards.a:
Survey it continually (like regulations, geopolitical, and market changes), assess impact on scope/backlog, and respond through governance:
• Evaluate with a time-boxed spike/POC before committing
• Reprioritize the backlog if AI shifts market value
• Ignore sunk cost when a new technology obsoletes work in progress

flashcards.q: When does a risk become an issue, and what changes?
flashcards.a:
When the risk's trigger occurs — the event is now certain/present.

Then: log it in the ISSUE log, execute/adapt the planned response, collaborate with stakeholders on resolution, and reassess for residual and secondary risks. You manage probability for risks; you manage resolution for issues.

flashcards.q: How do agile teams do risk management without a formal risk phase?
flashcards.a:
Continuously, through the mechanics of delivery:
• Short iterations limit exposure
• Risky/uncertain work is prioritized early (spikes)
• Reviews, boards, and radiators keep threats transparent
• Backlog reprioritization IS the risk response mechanism
Risk management is embedded, not absent.

flashcards.q: What is the pattern for ANY external change (regulation, tariff, market shift, technology, geopolitical event)?
flashcards.a:
1. ASSESS impact first (with experts — legal, compliance, finance)
2. Develop options with trade-offs
3. Route the decision through governance (change control) or the backlog (PO prioritization)
4. Communicate transparently

Never: ignore it, act unilaterally, or bypass process because the change feels urgent.

flashcards.q: How should the PM handle impediments per the 2026 ECO?
flashcards.a:
Evaluate impact → prioritize (widest/most severe blocker first) → apply an intervention to remove or minimize → reassess continually.

Recurring impediments are SYSTEM problems: root-cause the process, don't re-treat the symptom each sprint. Escalate only after genuine attempts, with impact data.

flashcards.q: What does 'support organizational change' require from the PM?
flashcards.a:
1. Assess organizational culture (before tailoring, before rollout)
2. Evaluate the impact of org change ON the project (reorgs, mergers, new sponsors → reassess stakeholders and commitments)
3. Drive change management FOR the project's outputs: readiness, communication, training — because outputs without adoption deliver no value.

flashcards.q: What are the PMP non-negotiables when compliance or safety failures surface?
flashcards.a:
• Imminent danger to people: STOP the work first, process second
• Verify facts before acting on alleged violations
• Present noncompliance consequences to the proper authority — never launch/ship quietly
• Never conceal, backdate, or structure around thresholds
Transparency at the right governance level is always the exam answer.
